vulnerabilities.title: JWT Tokens Exposed in WebSocket URL Query Parameters
vulnerabilities.description: JWT authentication tokens are passed as query parameters in WebSocket URLs (wss://domain/ws?token=JWT). This exposes sensitive tokens to server logs, browser history, network monitoring tools, and potential referrer leakage.

additional_locations.code: this._socket = new WebSocket(url);  // URL contains token in query string

vulnerabilities.impact: High - JWT tokens can be extracted from web server logs, proxy logs, browser debugging tools, and network traffic analysis. Attackers with access to logs can steal valid authentication tokens and impersonate users.

prerequisites: Access to server logs, proxy logs, or network monitoring tools
User must initiate WebSocket collaboration session
steps: Monitor WebSocket connection establishment (server logs, network capture)
Extract JWT token from query parameter: wss://api.example.com/ws?token=eyJhbGc...
Use extracted token to authenticate API requests or WebSocket connections
Impersonate victim user until token expires

references: CWE-598: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings

steps: Open browser developer tools (F12)
Authenticate to TMI application
Navigate to DFD collaboration feature
In Network tab, filter for WebSocket connections (WS/WSS)
Inspect WebSocket connection details
Verify token appears in URL query string: ?token=eyJhbGc...
test_case.expected_result: Token should NOT be visible in WebSocket URL. Should use alternative authentication method.

vulnerabilities.title: Open Redirect Vulnerability in Authentication Flow
vulnerabilities.description: The authentication flow accepts an unvalidated returnUrl parameter that is used to redirect users after successful login. An attacker can manipulate this parameter to redirect victims to malicious websites for phishing, session theft, or malware distribution.
vulnerabilities.impact: High - Attackers can redirect authenticated users to malicious websites that impersonate the application, harvest credentials, steal session tokens, or distribute malware. The redirect occurs after successful authentication, increasing user trust in the destination.

prerequisites: None - any user can be targeted
User must click crafted link and authenticate
steps: Craft malicious login URL: https://tmi-app.com/login?returnUrl=https://evil-tmi.com/phishing
Send link to victim via phishing email or social engineering
Victim clicks link and authenticates successfully
Application redirects victim to attacker's site: https://evil-tmi.com/phishing
Attacker's site displays fake "session expired" message
Victim re-enters credentials on attacker's phishing page
Attacker harvests victim's credentials

references: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
OWASP Top 10 2021 - A01: Broken Access Control
OWASP Cheat Sheet: Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards

steps: Logout of TMI application
Navigate to: https://tmi-app.com/login?returnUrl=https://evil.com
Complete authentication with valid credentials
Observe redirect destination after successful login
test_case.expected_result: Application should redirect to safe default page (dashboard/intake), NOT to https://evil.com
test_case.actual_result: Application redirects to https://evil.com - Open Redirect confirmed

vulnerabilities.title: WebSocket Token Expiration Not Handled
vulnerabilities.description: When JWT tokens are refreshed during an active WebSocket session, the WebSocket connection continues to use the original (potentially expired) token from the URL. There is no mechanism to update the WebSocket connection with the new token, leading to potential authentication failures or use of expired credentials.

location.code: // Token refresh triggered on WebSocket activity but connection not updated

additional_locations.code: // WebSocket URL created with token at connection time
// New token stored but WebSocket not notified

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium - WebSocket connections may fail authentication if backend invalidates old tokens on refresh. Long-running collaboration sessions could be interrupted. If backend doesn't invalidate old tokens, expired tokens remain active longer than intended.

prerequisites: Token refresh occurs during session (after 45 minutes)
steps: User establishes WebSocket collaboration session
Wait 45+ minutes for token to approach expiration
Token refresh occurs (new token stored in AuthService)
WebSocket continues using old token from URL
If backend invalidates old tokens: WebSocket authentication fails
Collaboration session breaks without automatic recovery

references: OWASP Top 10 2021 - A07: Identification and Authentication Failures

steps: Authenticate to TMI application
Start DFD collaboration session (WebSocket connection established)
Wait 50 minutes (past 15-minute refresh threshold)
Monitor browser console for token refresh events
Verify WebSocket connection continues functioning
Check if WebSocket uses old or new token
test_case.expected_result: WebSocket should reconnect with new token after refresh
test_case.actual_result: WebSocket continues using original token from URL

vulnerabilities.title: Relaxed OAuth State Validation for TMI Proxy
vulnerabilities.description: OAuth state parameter validation is relaxed when response.access_token is present, trusting the TMI server's state management. If an attacker can inject a response with an access_token, they might bypass CSRF checks that would normally validate the state parameter.

location.code: if (response.access_token) { /* Flexible state validation - trusts server */ }

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium - If an attacker can control the OAuth callback response and inject an access_token, they might bypass state parameter CSRF validation. However, this requires compromising the OAuth flow or the TMI backend server.

prerequisites: Ability to inject OAuth callback response with access_token
Compromise of TMI backend server OR man-in-the-middle position
Valid state parameter from legitimate flow (to bypass basic checks)
steps: Attacker initiates legitimate OAuth flow to obtain valid state parameter
Attacker crafts malicious callback with injected access_token
Frontend detects access_token presence and relaxes state validation
Attacker bypasses CSRF checks that would validate state parameter
Malicious authentication potentially accepted

references: OWASP Top 10 2021 - A01: Broken Access Control

steps: Review trust assumptions between frontend and TMI backend
Document OAuth proxy security model
Verify backend implements state validation
Confirm backend prevents token injection
Add tests for malicious callback responses

vulnerabilities.title: No SAML RelayState Validation
vulnerabilities.description: SAML authentication flows do not validate RelayState parameters. While returnUrl is stored in sessionStorage, there is no verification that the RelayState from the SAML response matches expectations or that the correct SAML provider completed the authentication.
vulnerabilities.impact: Low - Limited impact as SAML assertions are validated by backend. Frontend RelayState validation provides defense-in-depth but is not primary security control.

prerequisites: Backend SAML validation must also be bypassed

vulnerabilities.title: Development Mode Uses Unencrypted WebSocket
vulnerabilities.description: In development mode with HTTP API URLs, WebSocket connections use unencrypted ws:// protocol instead of wss://. This exposes JWT tokens and collaboration data in plaintext over the network.
vulnerabilities.impact: Medium (Development Only) - JWT tokens and collaboration data transmitted in plaintext during development. Not an issue in production if HTTPS is enforced, but could affect development security.

prerequisites: Network access to development machine (same network or MITM)
steps: Position on same network as developer
Sniff network traffic (e.g., Wireshark)
Capture ws:// WebSocket connections
Extract JWT token from WebSocket upgrade request
Extract collaboration data from WebSocket messages

references: CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

steps: Configure development environment with HTTP API URL
Start network packet capture (Wireshark/tcpdump)
Initiate WebSocket collaboration session
Filter for WebSocket traffic (port 80, ws://)
Verify plaintext JWT token in WebSocket upgrade request
test_case.expected_result: Development should use wss:// or warn about security risk
test_case.actual_result: Development uses ws:// with plaintext token transmission
